As can be seen the data fits well to parabolic behaviour especially at the lower temperatures.

Also shown are precipitates indicted by arrows. Boxes indicate the sites from which higher magnification and EDS maps were taken (Figs. 7 and 8).

Fig. 7 TEM images with EDS maps taken from the sample with a shot-peened surface finish exposed under isothermal conditions for 1025 h at 800 °C showing compositional and morphology details of two types of precipitates distributed throughout the alloy: a cubic shaped phase with a zirconium oxide centre surrounded by layers of Nb, Ta and Ti and a high aspect ratio phase of Nb and Ti aligned along dislocations pile-ups, higher nitrogen, carbon and boron signal compared to the matrix show these to be a mixture of nitrides, carbides and borides. See modified Fig. 8 below.

Fig. 8 TEM image with selected EDS maps taken from the sample with a polished surface exposed under isothermal conditions for 2000 h at 800 °C showing the interface between the unaffected γ/γ' region (right-hand side) and the oxidation affected region (left-hand side of images). Fig. 9 Transmission image of foil taken from the as-received alloy with selected EDS maps showing the distribution of elements in the matrix associated with the γ / γ' structure and the presence of titanium, tantalum and niobium associated with the carbon.

The hashed region shows the range of data for comparable alloys [5–19] with the lower boundary corresponding the formation of pure Cr₂O₃ [20–23]. The data up to 840°C lies towards the lower bound and the gradient gave a value of 269 kJ mol⁻¹ for the activation energy for the oxidation process for the development alloy. Over 840 °C the kinetics move towards the upper boundary.

Fig. 2 BSE images of the surface morphology of oxide scales formed on (a) NiCoCrAlY, (b) NiCoCrAlHf and (c) NiCoCrAlYHf after oxidation at 1100 °C for 500 h. High magnification BSE images in (c) and (d) show the RE-rich precipitates, as confirmed by the EDS point analysis.

Fig. 3 The evolution of oxide spallation degree with oxidation time at 1100 °C. The spallation degree was defined as the ratio of the spalled area to the whole surface area. The insets show the BSE images of surface morphology of NiCoCrAl and NiCoCrAlHf alloys after oxidation.

Fig. 4 Surface morphology of spalled regions in different alloys after oxidation at 1100 °C: (a) NiCoCrAlY and (b) NiCoCrAlHf for 500 h; (c, d) NiCoCrAl for 5 min and 423 h, respectively. By comparing to the microstructure of the as-cast alloy in Fig. 1, it is found that interfacial voids mainly form on β phase regions, as indicated by the white arrows in (c).

Fig. 5 Cross-sectional BSE micrographs of oxide scales formed on (a, d) NiCoCrAlY, (b, e) NiCoCrAlHf and (c, f) NiCoCrAlYHf samples after oxidation at 1100 °C for 500 h. The Ni protective layer was fabricated using electroplated method and the details can be seen elsewhere [41].

Fig. 6 Secondary electron (SE) images of the fractured cross-sectional morphology of oxide scales formed on (a) NiCoCrAl, (b) NiCoCrAlY, (c) NiCoCrAlHf and (d) NiCoCrAYHf after oxidation at 1100 °C. Lines A and B denote the total oxide scale thickness and columnar zone thickness, respectively. Thickness measurements were performed for ten pairs in each graph. The fractured cross-sections of NiCoCrAl and NiCoCrAlHf were taken from the spalled oxide scales. For the NiCoCrAlY and NiCoCrAlYHf, the fractured cross-sections were obtained by mechanical fracture. The oxidized samples were notched on the back by a sharp blade mounted in a precision cut-off machine and then broken to obtain the fractured cross-sections.

Fig. 7 TEM analysis of the oxide scale/metal interface in NiCoCrAlYHf after 500 h oxidation at 1100 °C: (a) HAADF STEM image giving an overview of the interface; (b) magnified view of the grain boundary in (a); (c) EDS mapping over the area in the orange rectangle in (a), showing segregation of Y and Hf to the Al₂O₃ grain boundary.

Fig. 8 Comparison of the growth of equiaxed and columnar layers between different alloys. (a) Schematic diagram showing oxidized samples after wedge polishing and then re-oxidizing. SE images of the surface morphology of polished wedge samples after re-oxidation at 1100 °C for 6 h: (b) NiCoCrAlY and (c) NiCoCrAlYHf. Linear fitting of the thickness of (d) equiaxed and (e) columnar layers with the square root of oxidation time based on Eq. (2). The white lines in (a) and (b) show the separation between oxides formed on polished alloy substrates (black arrows) and columnar grains (white arrows).
Fig. 9 Linear fitting of the oxide scale thickness versus the square root of oxidation time. The thickness was measured using ImageJ software based on the cross-sectional BSE images of oxide scales.

Fig. 10 Evolution of (a) residual stress and (b) growth stress in oxide scale as a function of oxidation time at 1100 ◦C. The insets in (a) and (b) show the residual stress and the growth stress, respectively, after short-time oxidation.

Fig. 11 TOF-SIMS measurements of S for (a, d) NiCoCrAlY, (b, e) NiCoCrAlHf and (c, f) NiCoCrAlYHf after oxidation at 1100 ◦C for 500 h. Images (a) to (c) were taken under the SE mode, and showed the area after the TOF-SIMS analysis. SIMS imaging for S was conducted on a square area at the cross-section of the oxide scale, and was acquired for about 250 s. White arrows in (e) indicate the S segregation to the detached interface in NiCoCrAlHf.

Fig. 12 Parabolic rate constants of different RE-doped alloys versus the activation energy (Q) for breaking the metal-oxygen bonds. The activation energy (Q) of the alloys doped with two REs is taken as the average value of Q of the alloys doped with either of the REs. The radii of RE ions (Å) are presented in Fig. 10. Note that the decreasing trend of k with Q is found in Y-, Hf- and YHf-doped NiCrAl in [13] and FeCrAl in [3], thus the results are not presented. To avoid the influence of severe internal oxidation caused by overdoping, the doping levels of each RE were controlled below 0.06 at% in either types of alloys.

Fig. 13 Variation of $\frac{k_{Undoped}}{k_{Y-doped}}$ as a function of $\frac{d_{Undoped}}{d_{Y-doped}}$ for three different $\frac{D_O^*(Undoped)}{D_O^*(Y-doped)}$ values. It was assumed that the thickness of the Al₂O₃ grain boundary (δ) was 1 nm. The ratio of lateral grain size is also indicated according to Fig. 6.

Fig. S1 Measurement of the oxide ridges volume on tapered (a-c) NiCoCrAlY and (d-f) NiCoCrAlYHf samples (pre-oxidized at 1100 °C for 100 h and then tapered by wedge polishing) after re-oxidizing for 6 h at 1100 °C. (a and d) BSE images of the surface morphology. (b and e) AFM scans over the area in the white rectangle in (a) and (d). (c and f) Processed images for quantifying the length of oxide ridges. Three regions, marked as 1, 2 and 3 in (b) and (e), were selected to measure the volume of oxide ridges.

Fig. S2 Al grain boundary flux as a function of the distance from the border between the original oxide and the newly formed oxide to the position where the volume of oxide ridges was measured for NiCoCrAlY and NiCoCrAlYHf samples. The samples were pre-oxidized for 100 h at 1100 °C, then re-oxidized for 6 h at 1100 °C.
